question.text: An investment adviser is helping a client, Karan (age 40), who has opted for the old tax regime and has exhausted his Section 80C limit of Rs.1,50,000. Karan wants additional tax savings. Which of the following recommendations is MOST appropriate?

answer.text: Invest Rs.1,00,000 more in PPF to exceed the 80C limit
Invest Rs.50,000 in NPS under Section 80CCD(1B) for an additional deduction beyond the 80CCE ceiling
Invest Rs.50,000 in ELSS for an additional deduction under 80CCD(1B)
Open a Sukanya Samriddhi Account for himself under Section 80CCD(1B)

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Since Karan has exhausted his Rs.1.5L limit under 80CCE (aggregate of 80C+80CCC+80CCD(1)), additional investments in PPF or ELSS will NOT yield further deductions. Section 80CCD(1B) provides an additional Rs.50,000 deduction specifically for NPS contributions — this is OVER AND ABOVE the Rs.1.5L ceiling. ELSS falls under 80C (not 80CCD(1B)), and Sukanya Samriddhi Account is for a girl child (not self). **The Trap:** Option A suggests more PPF but the 80C limit is already exhausted. Option C wrongly classifies ELSS under 80CCD(1B). Option D confuses Sukanya Samriddhi (for girl child under 80C) with NPS. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-B Investment Adviser exam specifically tests the ability to recommend appropriate tax-saving strategies beyond the basic 80C limit. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** After exhausting 80C, the next stop for tax savings should always be 80CCD(1B) for NPS — it is the most straightforward way to get an additional Rs.50,000 deduction under the old regime.
